course_description:
This integrative, capstone course is concerned with the interplay of finance strategy and organisational policy and strategy making.  How finance strategy supports the achievement of the strategic ambitions of organisations is a particular focus.  Key issues are establishing effective finance strategies and the organisational and environmental precursors to developing such strategies.  Emphasis is placed on the alignment of finance strategy with organisational strategy.  Students are expected to show intellectual leadership by conducting a significant research project within the domain of finance.

This course equips you with the skills to understand, synthesize, and critique research in finance. You will explore key research methods used in finance and learn to identify and evaluate high-quality studies. Additionally, the course covers current issues in finance, requiring extensive reading and critical analysis of relevant topics.

Course Changes in Response to Previous Student Feedback

For Semester 1 2026, the course has been updated to include more real-time demonstrations of STATA exercises during classes, along with additional hands‑on exercises incorporated into class time. The assessment weightings have also been adjusted, with a reduced percentage allocated to the literature review and an increased percentage allocated to the presentation.

restrictions: Capstone course to be studied in the major. Course is restricted to students enrolled in the BAdvBus(Hons) program.

assessment_summary.title: Academic Paper Presentation and Discussion
assessment_summary.due_date: Week 3 - Week 13; Presentations will be held during class

assessment_details.title: Academic Paper Presentation and Discussion
assessment_details.due_date: Week 3 - Week 13Presentations will be held during class
assessment_details.task_description: Presentation and Discussant TaskThis assessment consists of two components:Paper Presentation:Each student presents one academic paper from the reading list, providing a concise overview and a critical evaluation of its research design, empirical strategy, and approach to causal identification.Discussant Role (2 occasions):Each student will also act as discussant for two papers presented by peers.Presentation Requirements·      Explain the identification strategy (e.g., DiD, RD, IV, event studies) and how it is implemented.·      Discuss the appropriateness of the design and the credibility of its assumptions.·      Highlight strengths, limitations, and implications for causal interpretation.·      Reading from notes will be penalised.Lead Discussant Requirements·      Prepare two method‑focused questions for each assigned paper.·      Questions must address the research design, identification assumptions, empirical strategy, or robustness.·      General or superficial questions that indicate lack of engagement will receive lower marks.WeightingPresentation: 20 marksDiscussant: 5 marks totalPresentations and discussions will take place throughout the semester.AI Statement:Artificial Intelligence (AI) and Machine Translation (MT) are emerging tools that may support students in completing this assessment task. Students may appropriately use AI and/or MT in completing this assessment task. Students must clearly reference any use of AI or MT in each instance.A failure to reference generative AI or MT use may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct.Read full task descriptionHide full task description
assessment_details.submission_guidelines:
The focus of the presentation should be on the methods employed and how they relate to the results presented.
Every result discussed must be directly supported by the corresponding evidence in the table.
Presenters are required to engage with the material without reading from notes.Any reading from notes will incur a significant penalty,as the presentation must demonstrate understanding, critical interpretation, and clear communication.
Discussion should be limited to the results presented in the table, with commentary that is concise, analytical, and academically grounded.
A portion of the marks will be allocated to the discussant component. Each paper will have an assigned discussant, and each student will serve as discussant for two papers.
Deferral or extension
You cannot defer or apply for an extension for this assessment.
Extensions or deferrals are not available for this presentation
Late submission
Late submissions are not accepted for in-class presentations. Failure to present at the scheduled time will result in a mark of zero.

assessment_details.title: Literature Review & Research Proposal
assessment_details.task_description: For this assessment, you will need to form and register a group on BlackboardThe task involves preparing a structuredLiterature Review and Research Proposalon a topic selected by the group from the list of topics provided by the course coordinator. After conducting a preliminary review of the relevant literature, the group must first formulate a clear research question that aligns with the selected topic area.The literature review should focus primarily onpeer‑reviewed academic research, synthesising and critically evaluating the key studies relevant to the chosen topic. The review should map the development of the research area, identify major contributions, and highlight current debates, tensions, or unresolved questions within the existing literature. The emphasis should be on understanding how prior research has approached the topic, including the theoretical foundations, empirical strategies, and main findings.While the assessment must be grounded in academic scholarship, a limited number ofhigh‑quality non‑academic sourcesmay be used where they add valuable context or industry insight. Appropriate sources include reports from reputable investment banks, management consulting firms, international organisations, and established non‑profit institutions. The use of such sources should remain selective, and the academic literature must serve as the primary foundation of the review.The literature review should not simply summarise existing work but should critically examine conceptual gaps, methodological limitations, and areas where empirical evidence remains incomplete or contested. Building on this analysis, the assessment should conclude with a conciseresearch proposaloutlining a potential empirical study motivated by the group’s synthesis of the literature. The proposal should define a clear research question, articulate its academic and practical relevance, and propose a suitable empirical strategy. This includes identifying potential data sources and specifying an appropriate research design and identification approach capable of supporting credible causal inference.AI Statement:Artificial Intelligence (AI) and Machine Translation (MT) are emerging tools that may support students in completing this assessment task. Students may appropriately use AI and/or MT in completing this assessment task. Students must clearly reference any use of AI or MT in each instance.A failure to reference generative AI or MT use may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct.Read full task descriptionHide full task description
assessment_details.submission_guidelines:
Via Blackboard
Deferral or extension
You may be able toapply for an extension.
The maximum extension allowed is 14 days. Extensions are given in multiples of 24 hours.
Late submission
Apenaltyof 10% of the maximum possible mark will be deducted per 24 hours from time submission is due for up to 7 days. After 7 days, you will receive a mark of 0.

assessment_details.title: Empirical Research Analysis
assessment_details.other_conditions: Time limited, Secure.See the conditions definitions
assessment_details.task_description: This in‑person assessment requires the application of empirical research skills to real-world data. Students will analyse relationships between key variables using descriptive statistics and appropriate econometric techniques. The task assesses the ability to implement regression‑based methods, interpret empirical results, and evaluate the credibility of the evidence produced.In line with the course’s focus, students will be required to applycausal research techniquesdiscussed throughout the semester. This may include the use of Difference‑in‑Differences (DiD), Regression Discontinuity (RD), Instrumental Variables (IV), event-study designs, or other identification strategies. Emphasis is placed on implementing empirical methods within a clear research design and interpreting the resulting evidence in a rigorous and coherent manner.Further details will be provided on Blackboard and discussed in class.AI Statement:Artificial Intelligence (AI) and Machine Translation (MT) are emerging tools that may support students in completing this assessment task. Students may appropriately use AI and/or MT in completing this assessment task. Students must clearly reference any use of AI or MT in each instance.A failure to reference generative AI or MT use may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct.Read full task descriptionHide full task description
